title: 06. Multi-agent systems
tagline: Play supervisor: route subtasks to specialist agents and ship a feature as a team.
description: The supervisor pattern — why routed teams of narrow agents beat one do-everything agent, how to decompose goals, and when multi-agent is overkill.

exercises.title: Why teams beat heroes
exercises.content:
# Why teams beat heroes

Give one agent twelve tools and a vague goal, and you get the AI version of the employee with twelve jobs: mediocre at all of them, impossible to debug.

Multi-agent systems fix this the way organizations do — **specialization plus coordination**.

### The supervisor pattern

The simplest reliable architecture:

```
            SUPERVISOR
     (decomposes, routes, verifies)
       /         |          \
  RESEARCHER   BUILDER    REVIEWER
  (few tools) (few tools) (few tools)
```

- The **supervisor** owns the goal. It splits work into subtasks, routes each to a specialist, and checks results. Crucially, *it does no work itself* — a supervisor that also codes is just the twelve-tool hero again.
- Each **specialist** has a narrow prompt, a short tool list, and one job. Narrow agents route tools better (course 05!), hallucinate less, and their traces are readable.

### Why the separation matters

**Focused context.** Each specialist's context window carries only its own task — no cross-contamination from twelve unrelated tools and histories.

**Independent review.** A builder reviewing its own work inherits its own blind spots. A separate reviewer with fresh context catches what the author can't — same reason code review exists.

**Debuggability.** When the output is wrong, you can see *which* agent failed. One mega-agent gives you a 400-step trace and a headache.

### The honest trade-offs
- **More model calls** — every hop costs tokens and latency.
- **Handoff loss** — information can get mangled between agents; handoffs need structured formats (course 03's schemas again).
- **Overkill for simple tasks** — one agent with three tools beats a five-agent committee for a lookup. Reach for multi-agent when tasks genuinely need different specialties or independent verification.

Next: you take the supervisor chair. Route three subtasks to the right specialists and ship a feature.
exercises.attribution: Adapted from the Hugging Face Agents Course (Apache-2.0) and "AI Agents for Beginners" by Microsoft (MIT)

exercises.title: Video: Multi-agent design patterns
exercises.content:
### Watch: Multi-agent design patterns

From Microsoft's *AI Agents for Beginners*. Watch for:

- When they recommend multi-agent over single-agent (and when they don't).
- How **handoffs** carry state between agents.
- The supervisor/orchestrator's job description.

exercises.title: Playground: Mission control
exercises.content:
# Mission control

Your team is shipping a **salary-comparison feature**: a Researcher, a Builder, and a Reviewer await orders. Three subtasks arrive in sequence — route each to the right specialist.

Misroute freely: the agents will politely refuse and tell you why the task isn't theirs. (Note the Builder declining to review its own code — that instinct is the whole reason the Reviewer exists.)

*To complete this lesson: ship the feature.*

exercises.title: Knowledge check: orchestration
exercises.content:
# Knowledge check

Three questions. All three to pass.

quiz.prompt: In the supervisor pattern, what should the supervisor NOT do?
options: Perform the subtasks itself when the specialists seem slow
quiz.explanation: The moment the supervisor starts doing the work, you're back to one overloaded agent — with extra steps. Routing and verification are the whole job.

quiz.prompt: Why give review to a separate agent instead of asking the builder to check its own work?
options: The builder's context is too full
An author re-reading its own output inherits its own blind spots; fresh context catches different errors
Reviewer models are a special type of LLM
quiz.explanation: Same model, fresh context, adversarial framing — that separation is what makes review meaningful. It's code review for agents.

quiz.prompt: A user wants to look up one fact from your docs. Multi-agent or single agent?
options: Multi-agent — more agents means more reliability
Single agent (or plain RAG) — multi-agent adds calls, latency, and handoff risk for zero benefit here
quiz.explanation: Architecture should match task complexity. Simple lookup = simple pipeline. Save the team for work that needs distinct specialties or verification.

exercises.title: Code lab: Assign the specialist
exercises.content:
# Assign the specialist

You routed by instinct in mission control — now encode the routing rules, exactly what a supervisor prompt does under the hood.

### Your task
Implement `assignAgent(task)` and return `researcher`, `builder`, `reviewer`, or `supervisor`.

### Source bench
- Hugging Face Agents Course
- Microsoft AI Agents for Beginners
exercises.hint: collect/sources means researcher; implement/build means builder; audit/review means reviewer.
